questions.stem: What does the existence of a Vitali set establish?
options: That there exists a subset of $\mathbb{R}$ that is NOT Lebesgue measurable.
That every subset of $\mathbb{R}$ is Lebesgue measurable.
That Lebesgue measure is identically zero.
That the Borel sets exhaust the power set.
questions.explanation: Vitali's construction produces a concrete subset of $\mathbb{R}$ to which Lebesgue measure cannot be consistently assigned, showing that not every set is measurable. This is the standard demonstration that the measurable $\sigma$-algebra $\mathcal{M}$ is strictly smaller than the full power set.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)

questions.stem: The construction of a Vitali set relies essentially on which axiom?
options: No axioms beyond the field axioms.
questions.explanation: A Vitali set is built by choosing one representative from each coset of $\mathbb{Q}$ in $\mathbb{R}$, which requires selecting simultaneously from uncountably many nonempty sets, exactly the axiom of choice. Without choice it is consistent (the Solovay model) that all subsets of $\mathbb{R}$ are measurable.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)

questions.stem: In the Vitali construction, two real numbers $x$ and $y$ are placed in the same equivalence class when:
options: $x - y \in \mathbb{Q}$, that is they differ by a rational.
questions.explanation: The relevant equivalence relation declares $x \sim y$ iff $x - y$ is rational, partitioning $\mathbb{R}$ into cosets of the subgroup $\mathbb{Q}$. A Vitali set is then formed by choosing exactly one representative from each such coset, which is where the axiom of choice enters.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)

questions.stem: A Vitali set $V \subseteq [0,1]$ is built by selecting from each coset:
questions.explanation: By definition $V$ contains exactly one point from each equivalence class of the relation $x \sim y \iff x - y \in \mathbb{Q}$. Choosing one representative per class for uncountably many classes is precisely the step that invokes the axiom of choice.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)

questions.stem: The two properties of Lebesgue measure that, taken together, are contradicted by a measurable Vitali set are:
questions.explanation: If $V$ were measurable, the disjoint rational translates $V + q$ for $q \in \mathbb{Q} \cap [-1,1]$ would all have the same measure by translation invariance, and countable additivity applied to their union would force that common measure to be both $0$ and positive, a contradiction. So no measure can be countably additive and translation invariant on all sets.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)

questions.stem: In the contradiction, the countably many translates $V + q$ (for rationals $q$ in a bounded range) are:
questions.explanation: If $x \in (V + q_1) \cap (V + q_2)$ then two representatives of the same coset would differ by the rational $q_1 - q_2$, contradicting that $V$ has exactly one representative per coset unless $q_1 = q_2$. Hence distinct rational translates of $V$ are pairwise disjoint, which is what makes countable additivity bite.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)

questions.stem: If the Vitali set $V$ had measure $m(V) = 0$, what contradiction arises from its rational translates covering $[0,1]$?
options: The union would have infinite measure.
The union would have measure $0$, yet it contains $[0,1]$ which has measure $1$.
The translates would not be disjoint.
questions.explanation: A countable union of disjoint measure-zero translates would have measure $\sum_q 0 = 0$ by countable additivity, but this union contains $[0,1]$, forcing its measure to be at least $1$. The clash $0 \geq 1$ is impossible, so $m(V) = 0$ cannot hold.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)

questions.stem: If the Vitali set $V$ had positive measure $m(V) = c > 0$, what contradiction arises?
options: The bounded union of countably many disjoint copies of measure $c$ would have infinite measure, exceeding the measure of a bounded set.
questions.explanation: Countably many disjoint translates, each of measure $c > 0$, would give the union measure $\sum_q c = \infty$ by countable additivity. But the union sits inside a bounded interval such as $[-1, 2]$, whose measure is finite, so $\infty \leq 3$ is impossible. Hence $m(V)$ cannot be positive either.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)

questions.stem: Putting the two cases together, the Vitali set $V$ cannot have measure $0$ and cannot have positive measure. The conclusion is that:
questions.explanation: Both $m(V) = 0$ and $m(V) > 0$ lead to contradictions, so $V$ cannot be assigned any measure consistently; that is, $V$ is non-measurable. The argument never produces a numerical value, precisely because measurability itself fails.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)

questions.stem: Which countable group is used as the translation group in the Vitali construction on $\mathbb{R}$?
questions.explanation: The construction uses cosets of the additive subgroup $\mathbb{Q} \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, and the translates of $V$ are by rationals. The key features needed are that $\mathbb{Q}$ is a countable dense subgroup, which both the integers (not dense) and the irrationals (not a group) fail to provide.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)

questions.stem: Why is it important that $\mathbb{Q}$ is countable in the Vitali argument?
options: Because uncountable groups have no cosets.
So that the translates can be summed using countable additivity of measure.
It is not important; any group works.
questions.explanation: Countability of $\mathbb{Q}$ means there are only countably many translates $V + q$, so countable additivity (not just finite additivity) governs the measure of their union, driving the contradiction. An uncountable index set would put the argument outside the reach of countable additivity.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)

questions.stem: A non-measurable set such as a Vitali set is a subset of $\mathbb{R}$ for which:
options: the Caratheodory splitting condition $m^*(A) = m^*(A \cap V) + m^*(A \cap V^c)$ fails for some test set $A$.
questions.explanation: Outer measure $m^*$ is defined for every subset of $\mathbb{R}$, including Vitali sets, but a non-measurable set is one where the Caratheodory criterion fails: there is a test set $A$ that is split strictly subadditively. Uncountability alone does not cause non-measurability, since $[0,1]$ is uncountable and measurable.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)

questions.stem: Does a Vitali set have a well-defined Lebesgue outer measure $m^*(V)$?
options: Yes, outer measure is defined for every subset of $\mathbb{R}$; only the Caratheodory additivity fails.
No, outer measure is undefined for it.
questions.explanation: Lebesgue outer measure assigns a value to every set, so $m^*(V)$ exists (it is some positive number). What fails for a Vitali set is not the existence of $m^*(V)$ but the additive splitting required by the Caratheodory criterion, which is why $V$ is non-measurable rather than 'unmeasured'.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)

questions.stem: Which statement about non-measurable sets is TRUE?
options: Non-measurable sets exist, but exhibiting one requires the axiom of choice.
questions.explanation: Non-measurable sets do exist, but all known constructions invoke the axiom of choice, and indeed it is consistent without choice that none exist. Countable sets are null hence measurable, open sets are Borel hence measurable, and boundedness has no bearing on measurability.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)

questions.stem: The deep reason a fully translation-invariant, countably additive measure assigning $m([0,1]) = 1$ cannot measure ALL subsets of $\mathbb{R}$ is that:
options: such a measure would have to be identically zero.
these three requirements are jointly inconsistent on the full power set, as the Vitali set shows.
questions.explanation: Vitali's theorem shows there is no countably additive, translation-invariant measure defined on every subset of $\mathbb{R}$ that normalises $[0,1]$ to $1$. Lebesgue measure resolves this by restricting its domain to the measurable $\sigma$-algebra rather than the entire power set.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)

questions.stem: If we only demanded FINITE additivity (not countable), could a translation-invariant finitely additive measure on all subsets of $\mathbb{R}$ exist?
options: It is the same as Lebesgue measure.
Finite additivity forces the measure to be zero.
No finitely additive measure exists at all.
Such finitely additive extensions do exist on $\mathbb{R}$, so it is specifically countable additivity that clashes with the Vitali construction.
questions.explanation: Translation-invariant finitely additive measures on all subsets of $\mathbb{R}$ do exist (using amenability of the group), so finite additivity alone is not in conflict with the Vitali set. The contradiction in Vitali's argument specifically uses countable additivity over the countably many rational translates.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)

questions.stem: Is a Vitali set countable or uncountable?
options: Uncountable, because there are uncountably many cosets of $\mathbb{Q}$ in $\mathbb{R}$.
Countable, since it picks one point per class.
questions.explanation: Each coset is a countable set (a translate of $\mathbb{Q}$), so to cover the uncountable real line there must be uncountably many cosets, and $V$ has one representative from each. Hence $V$ is uncountable. If $V$ were countable it would be null and measurable, contradicting its construction.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)

questions.stem: Which of the following sets is measurable, in contrast to a Vitali set?
options: The union of all distinct rational translates of a Vitali set, restricted so they stay non-measurable.
A coset selection set under the axiom of choice.
questions.explanation: The Cantor set is closed, hence Borel and measurable (in fact with measure zero). A translate of a Vitali set is still non-measurable, and choice-built coset selectors are the very source of non-measurability, so only the Cantor set is measurable here.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)

questions.stem: The cosets of $\mathbb{Q}$ in $\mathbb{R}$ used in Vitali's construction are best described as:
options: uncountably many pairwise disjoint sets, each a translate of $\mathbb{Q}$, whose union is $\mathbb{R}$.
questions.explanation: The relation $x \sim y \iff x - y \in \mathbb{Q}$ partitions $\mathbb{R}$ into disjoint equivalence classes, each of the form $x + \mathbb{Q}$, a translate of the rationals. There are uncountably many such classes, and together they tile all of $\mathbb{R}$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)

questions.stem: Banach-Tarski-type paradoxes in higher dimensions are conceptually related to Vitali's example because both:
options: avoid the axiom of choice entirely.
show that Lebesgue measure is inconsistent.
exploit the axiom of choice to build sets that defy a naive, fully defined notion of volume or measure.
questions.explanation: Both the Vitali set and the Banach-Tarski paradox use the axiom of choice to produce non-measurable sets that frustrate any attempt to assign a consistent translation/rotation-invariant measure to all sets. They do not show Lebesgue measure is inconsistent; rather they show its domain must exclude such pathological sets.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)

questions.stem: Which statement is FALSE?
options: Every subset of $\mathbb{R}$ is Lebesgue measurable.
The construction of a non-measurable set uses the axiom of choice.
Non-measurability means the Caratheodory criterion fails for some test set.
questions.explanation: The false statement is that every subset of $\mathbb{R}$ is measurable; the Vitali set is a counterexample. The other three statements correctly describe Vitali sets, the role of the axiom of choice, and the meaning of non-measurability via the Caratheodory criterion.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)

questions.stem: Why can we assume a Vitali set $V$ is contained in a bounded interval such as $[0,1]$?
options: Because every coset of $\mathbb{Q}$ meets $[0,1]$, so a representative can be chosen there.
questions.explanation: Since $\mathbb{Q}$ is dense, each coset $x + \mathbb{Q}$ intersects $[0,1]$, so for every class we may pick a representative lying in $[0,1]$, keeping $V \subseteq [0,1]$ bounded. This boundedness is what later lets us trap the union of translates inside a finite-measure interval.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)

questions.stem: The union $\bigcup_{q \in \mathbb{Q} \cap [-1,1]} (V + q)$ in Vitali's argument is sandwiched between which two sets?
options: $[0,1]$ on the inside and $[-1, 2]$ on the outside.
questions.explanation: With $V \subseteq [0,1]$ and rational shifts $q \in [-1,1]$, the union contains $[0,1]$ (every point of $[0,1]$ is some representative plus a rational in range) and is contained in $[-1,2]$. The finite measures $1$ and $3$ of these bounding intervals force a contradiction whether $m(V)$ is zero or positive.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)

questions.stem: Without the axiom of choice, what is the status of non-measurable sets?
options: They always exist regardless of axioms.
Lebesgue measure cannot be defined at all.
It is consistent (Solovay's model) that every subset of $\mathbb{R}$ is Lebesgue measurable.
questions.explanation: Solovay showed that, relative to a suitable consistency assumption, there is a model of ZF (without the full axiom of choice) in which every subset of $\mathbb{R}$ is Lebesgue measurable. So the existence of non-measurable sets is not a theorem of ZF alone; it genuinely depends on choice.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)

questions.stem: Which property does a Vitali set $V$ still possess despite being non-measurable?
options: It has a well-defined positive outer measure $m^*(V) > 0$.
questions.explanation: Even though $V$ is non-measurable, outer measure is defined for all sets, and $m^*(V) > 0$ (if it were zero, $V$ would be null hence measurable). $V$ is uncountable and not open, so the only correct surviving property listed is its positive outer measure.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)

questions.stem: Which single change to the hypotheses would PREVENT the Vitali contradiction, allowing all sets to be measured?
options: Keeping countable additivity but dropping translation invariance is not enough by itself; one must also weaken additivity or the domain.
Requiring the measure to be defined only on a $\sigma$-algebra strictly smaller than the power set, namely $\mathcal{M}$.
Demanding the measure be uncountably additive.
questions.explanation: Lebesgue's resolution is to restrict the domain: by defining measure only on the $\sigma$-algebra $\mathcal{M}$ of measurable sets (which excludes Vitali sets) one keeps both countable additivity and translation invariance without contradiction. Trying to keep all sets in the domain forces giving up one of the other properties.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)

questions.stem: The Vitali equivalence relation and choice. Fill in the construction.
questions.solution: Vitali partitions $\mathbb{R}$ by declaring $x \sim y$ when the difference $x - y$ is {{1}}. To form the set $V$, one selects exactly one {{2}} from each equivalence class, a step that requires the axiom of {{3}}.
questions.explanation: The relation is $x \sim y \iff x - y \in \mathbb{Q}$, so the difference is rational. Choosing one representative per class for uncountably many classes uses the axiom of choice.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)

questions.stem: The two properties in tension. Identify what fails if a Vitali set were measurable.
questions.solution: Suppose $V$ were measurable. Its rational translates $V + q$ are pairwise {{1}} and all share the same measure by translation {{2}}. Summing their measures over the countably many rationals using countable {{3}} then yields a value that is simultaneously zero and positive, a contradiction.
questions.explanation: Distinct rational translates of $V$ are disjoint, and translation invariance gives them equal measure. Countable additivity then forces the bounded union to have measure both $0$ (if $m(V)=0$) and $\infty$ (if $m(V)>0$), which is impossible.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)

questions.stem: True or false about non-measurable sets.
questions.solution: Outer measure $m^*$ is defined for every subset of $\mathbb{R}$, including a Vitali set: this statement is {{1}}. A Vitali set is countable: this statement is {{2}}. The existence of a non-measurable set can be proved in ZF without any form of the axiom of choice: this statement is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Outer measure is defined on all subsets, so the first is true. A Vitali set is uncountable (it has one point from each of uncountably many cosets), so the second is false. By Solovay's model, ZF alone does not prove non-measurable sets exist, so the third is false.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)

questions.stem: Bounding the union of translates. Take $V \subseteq [0,1]$ and shifts $q \in \mathbb{Q} \cap [-1,1]$.
questions.solution: The union of the translates contains the interval $[0,1]$, whose measure is the number {{1}}, and is contained in the interval $[-1,2]$, whose measure is the number {{2}}. A bounded set trapped between these cannot have a countably additive total of infinitely many equal positive pieces, so $V$ must be {{3}}.
questions.explanation: The interval $[0,1]$ has measure $1$ and $[-1,2]$ has measure $3$. Since the union is squeezed between measure $1$ and measure $3$, neither $m(V)=0$ nor $m(V)>0$ is consistent, so $V$ is non-measurable.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)

questions.stem: Measurable versus non-measurable. Decide measurability of each set.
questions.solution: The Cantor set is Lebesgue measurable: answer {{1}}. A Vitali set is Lebesgue measurable: answer {{2}}. Every open subset of $\mathbb{R}$ is Lebesgue measurable: answer {{3}}.
questions.explanation: The Cantor set is closed, hence measurable, and open sets are Borel, hence measurable. A Vitali set is the standard non-measurable example, so it is not measurable.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Real Functions I course (MATH 461, measure theory)
